title: Check if two trees are identical
description: Check if two binary trees are structurally identical and have the same node values

examples.explanation:
Step 1: Compare root nodes (1 vs 1) → values match
Step 2: Recursively check left subtrees (2 vs 2) → values match
Step 3: Check left subtree's children (null vs null) → both null, return True
Step 4: Check right subtree's children (null vs null) → both null, return True
Step 5: Back to node 2: left=True, right=True → return True
Step 6: Recursively check right subtrees (3 vs 3) → values match
Step 7: Check left children (null vs null) → both null, return True
Step 8: Check right children (null vs null) → both null, return True
Step 9: Back to node 3: left=True, right=True → return True
Step 10: Back to root: left=True, right=True → return True

Step 1: Compare roots (1 vs 1) → values match
Step 2: Check left subtrees (2 vs null) → one is node, one is null → return False
Step 3: Since left=False, no need to check right subtree (short-circuit evaluation)
Step 4: Root receives left=False → return False

approaches.name: Approach 1: Recursive Depth-First Search
approaches.summary: Recursively compare nodes of both trees by checking if they are both null, have the same value, and their left and right subtrees are identical. This leverages the tree's recursive structure for a straightforward solution.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is the height of the tree (due to recursion stack)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Recursive DFS Tree Identity Check
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to determine if two binary trees are structurally identical with matching node values.
ladders.explanation:
This problem requires verifying that two binary trees are identical in both structure and content. The recursive DFS approach elegantly leverages the tree's hierarchical nature: two trees are identical if their root nodes match (both null or same value) AND their left subtrees are identical AND their right subtrees are identical. This creates a natural divide-and-conquer strategy where the problem reduces to smaller subproblems.

Intuition: Trees are recursive data structures - a tree consists of a root with left and right subtrees. If we can verify identity at the root level and recursively verify identity of subtrees, we solve the entire problem. The base cases handle null nodes elegantly: if both nodes are null, they're identical; if only one is null, they differ.

Algorithm:
1. Base case 1: If both nodes are null → return True (identical empty trees)
2. Base case 2: If one node is null but other isn't → return False (structural mismatch)
3. Value check: If node values differ → return False (content mismatch)
4. Recursive case: Return (recursive call on left subtrees) AND (recursive call on right subtrees)

Pseudocode:
function isIdentical(node1, node2):
    if node1 is null and node2 is null: return True
    if node1 is null or node2 is null: return False
    if node1.value != node2.value: return False
    return isIdentical(node1.left, node2.left) and isIdentical(node1.right, node2.right)

Time Complexity: O(n) where n is number of nodes in the smaller tree (we traverse each node once)
Space Complexity: O(h) where h is tree height (recursion stack depth)

Connection to L1: This full solution directly uses the recursive comparison function described in L1 as its core engine.

Common Mistakes:
1. Forgetting to check both null cases separately - leads to null pointer errors
2. Checking values before null checks - accessing value on null node crashes
3. Using OR instead of AND for recursive calls - would return True if any subtree matches
4. Not handling single null case properly - need explicit check for one null, one non-null

Real Code Pattern:
def isSameTree(p, q):
    if not p and not q:
        return True
    if not p or not q:
        return False
    if p.val != q.val:
        return False
    return isSameTree(p.left, q.left) and isSameTree(p.right, q.right)

examples.trace:
Step 1: Compare root nodes (1 vs 1) → values match
Step 2: Recursively check left subtrees (2 vs 2) → values match
Step 3: Check left subtree's children (null vs null) → both null, return True
Step 4: Check right subtree's children (null vs null) → both null, return True
Step 5: Back to node 2: left=True, right=True → return True
Step 6: Recursively check right subtrees (3 vs 3) → values match
Step 7: Check left children (null vs null) → both null, return True
Step 8: Check right children (null vs null) → both null, return True
Step 9: Back to node 3: left=True, right=True → return True
Step 10: Back to root: left=True, right=True → return True

Step 1: Compare roots (1 vs 1) → values match
Step 2: Check left subtrees (2 vs null) → one is node, one is null → return False
Step 3: Since left=False, no need to check right subtree (short-circuit evaluation)
Step 4: Root receives left=False → return False

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Node Comparison Function
ladders.desc: Implement the core recursive function that compares two tree nodes and their subtrees.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine is the heart of the tree identity check. It defines how to compare two nodes in a binary tree recursively. The function takes two tree nodes as input and returns True only if: (1) both nodes are null (empty trees match), OR (2) both nodes exist with identical values AND their left subtrees are identical AND their right subtrees are identical.

Intuition: Tree identity is defined recursively: two trees are identical if their roots match and their subtrees are identical. This creates a natural recursive definition where the problem for the whole tree reduces to the same problem for smaller subtrees.

Algorithm:
1. Handle the null cases first (safest approach)
2. If both inputs are null → return True (base case: empty trees are identical)
3. If exactly one is null → return False (structural mismatch)
4. Compare node values → if different, return False
5. Recursively compare left children
6. Recursively compare right children
7. Return logical AND of left and right results

Pseudocode:
function compareNodes(nodeA, nodeB):
    if nodeA == null and nodeB == null: return True
    if nodeA == null or nodeB == null: return False
    if nodeA.value != nodeB.value: return False
    leftMatch = compareNodes(nodeA.left, nodeB.left)
    rightMatch = compareNodes(nodeA.right, nodeB.right)
    return leftMatch and rightMatch

Time Complexity: O(min(n,m)) where n,m are node counts (stops at first mismatch)
Space Complexity: O(min(h1,h2)) for recursion stack

Connection to L0: This function IS the solution to L0 - the full problem simply calls this function with the tree roots.

Common Mistakes:
1. Checking values before null checks → null pointer exception
2. Using OR logic instead of AND → would match if any subtree matches
3. Not handling the 'one null, one not' case explicitly
4. Forgetting to propagate recursive results upward

Real Code Pattern:
def compare_nodes(p, q):
    # Handle null cases first
    if p is None and q is None:
        return True
    if p is None or q is None:
        return False
    # Now safe to access values
    if p.val != q.val:
        return False
    # Recursively check subtrees
    return compare_nodes(p.left, q.left) and compare_nodes(p.right, q.right)

examples.input: NodeA: 5 (with left=3, right=7), NodeB: 5 (with left=3, right=7)
examples.trace:
Step 1: Both nodes exist (not null)
Step 2: Values match (5=5)
Step 3: Recursively check left children (3 vs 3)
Step 4: Values match (3=3)
Step 5: Check left of node 3 (null vs null) → True
Step 6: Check right of node 3 (null vs null) → True
Step 7: Node 3 returns True (left=True, right=True)
Step 8: Recursively check right children (7 vs 7)
Step 9: Values match (7=7)
Step 10: Check left of node 7 (null vs null) → True
Step 11: Check right of node 7 (null vs null) → True
Step 12: Node 7 returns True
Step 13: Root returns True (left=True, right=True)

examples.input: NodeA: 5 (with left=3, right=null), NodeB: 5 (with left=null, right=3)
examples.trace:
Step 1: Both nodes exist
Step 2: Values match (5=5)
Step 3: Check left children (3 vs null)
Step 4: One is node, one is null → return False
Step 5: Since left=False, short-circuit evaluation skips right check
Step 6: Root receives left=False → return False

testCases.input: NodeA: Node(5, left=Node(3)), NodeB: Node(5, left=Node(3))
NodeA: Node(5, left=Node(3)), NodeB: Node(5, left=Node(4))
NodeA: Node(5, left=Node(3)), NodeB: Node(5, right=Node(3))
NodeA: Node(5, left=Node(3), right=Node(7)), NodeB: Node(5, left=Node(3), right=Node(7))
NodeA: Node(5, left=Node(3), right=Node(7)), NodeB: Node(5, left=Node(7), right=Node(3))

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Tree Traversal with Comparison
ladders.desc: Implement simultaneous depth-first traversal of two trees while comparing nodes.
ladders.explanation:
This level focuses on the traversal pattern that enables tree comparison. The core insight is that we need to traverse both trees in sync - visiting corresponding nodes at the same time. Depth-first search (specifically pre-order traversal) works perfectly because we compare the current node first, then recursively compare left and right subtrees.

Intuition: To compare two trees, we must visit them in the same order. Pre-order traversal (root, left, right) allows us to compare nodes as we encounter them. The recursion naturally ensures we compare corresponding nodes: when we're at node A in tree1 and node B in tree2, their left children correspond to each other, as do their right children.

Algorithm:
1. Start at the roots of both trees
2. At each step, compare the current nodes
3. If comparison fails, return False immediately (pruning)
4. If comparison passes, recursively traverse left subtrees together
5. Then recursively traverse right subtrees together
6. Only return True if all comparisons pass

Pseudocode:
function traverseAndCompare(node1, node2):
    # Comparison logic here
    # Recursive calls to traverse subtrees

Time Complexity: O(n) for full traversal
Space Complexity: O(h) for recursion stack

Connection to L1: This traversal pattern is implemented within the compareNodes function from L1.

Common Mistakes:
1. Traversing trees independently instead of in sync
2. Not using short-circuit evaluation to stop early on mismatch
3. Choosing wrong traversal order (post-order or in-order would work but are less intuitive)
4. Not maintaining correspondence between nodes in different trees

Real Code Pattern:
def traverse_compare(p, q):
    # This is the traversal framework
    if not p and not q:
        return True
    if not p or not q:
        return False
    # Pre-order: check current node first
    if p.val != q.val:
        return False
    # Then traverse left subtrees
    left_ok = traverse_compare(p.left, q.left)
    if not left_ok:
        return False  # Short-circuit
    # Then traverse right subtrees
    right_ok = traverse_compare(p.right, q.right)
    return right_ok

examples.trace:
Step 1: Visit roots (1,1) → match
Step 2: Go left to (2,2) → match
Step 3: Go left from 2 → (null,null) → match
Step 4: Back to 2, go right → (null,null) → match
Step 5: Back to root, go right to (3,3) → match
Step 6: Go left from 3 → (null,null) → match
Step 7: Go right from 3 → (null,null) → match
Step 8: All comparisons pass → return True

Step 1: Visit roots (1,1) → match
Step 2: Go left to (2,3) → values differ (2≠3) → return False
Step 3: Traversal stops immediately (short-circuit)

ladders.title: Building Block — Binary Tree Node Structure and Navigation
ladders.desc: Understand and work with binary tree node structure, accessing children and values.
ladders.explanation:
This level establishes the fundamental understanding of binary tree nodes, which are the building blocks of our comparison algorithm. A binary tree node typically contains: (1) a value/data field, (2) a reference/pointer to left child, (3) a reference/pointer to right child. The node may also have a parent reference, but for this problem we only need children references.

Intuition: Binary trees are recursive structures where each node is itself the root of a subtree. Understanding how to access a node's value and navigate to its children is essential for any tree algorithm. The null references represent missing children (leaf nodes have two null children).

Key Operations:
1. Access node value: node.value or node.val
2. Access left child: node.left
3. Access right child: node.right
4. Check if node is null/None (no node exists)
5. Check if node is leaf: node.left == null and node.right == null

Algorithm for Basic Navigation:
1. Start at root node
2. To go left: current = current.left
3. To go right: current = current.right
4. Check if can move: if current != null then can move

Time Complexity: O(1) for each access
Space Complexity: O(1) for navigation

Connection to L2: The traversal in L2 requires precisely these navigation operations to move through the tree.

Common Mistakes:
1. Accessing node.value on a null node → null pointer exception
2. Assuming all nodes have both children (binary trees can be incomplete)
3. Not checking for null before accessing children
4. Confusing node references with node values

Real Code Pattern:
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
        self.val = val
        self.left = left
        self.right = right

# Navigation examples:
node = TreeNode(5)
value = node.val  # Access value
left_child = node.left  # Access left child (may be None)
right_child = node.right  # Access right child
is_leaf = node.left is None and node.right is None
has_left = node.left is not None
has_right = node.right is not None

examples.input: Node with value 10, left child with value 5, right child with value 15
examples.trace:
Step 1: Create root node with value 10
Step 2: Create left child node with value 5
Step 3: Create right child node with value 15
Step 4: Set root.left = left child node
Step 5: Set root.right = right child node
Step 6: Access root.value = 10
Step 7: Access root.left.value = 5
Step 8: Access root.right.value = 15
Step 9: Check root.left.left = null
Step 10: Check root.left.right = null
Step 11: Check root.right.left = null
Step 12: Check root.right.right = null

Step 1: Create node with value 7
Step 2: node.left = null
Step 3: node.right = null
Step 4: node.value = 7
Step 5: Check if leaf: node.left==null and node.right==null → True
Step 6: Check if has left child: node.left!=null → False
Step 7: Check if has right child: node.right!=null → False

testCases.input: Node: value=100, left=Node(50), right=Node(150, left=Node(125), right=Node(175))

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Value Comparison and Null Checking
ladders.desc: Compare values and check for null/none states safely.
ladders.explanation:
This fundamental operation involves two critical checks: (1) comparing values for equality, and (2) checking if references are null (None in Python). These are the atomic operations that form the basis of our tree comparison. The order matters tremendously: we must check for null BEFORE accessing values to avoid null pointer exceptions.

Intuition: In tree algorithms, we often need to compare nodes. However, nodes can be null (representing missing children or empty trees). We need a safe comparison pattern: first check if both are null (equal), then check if one is null (unequal), then (only when both are non-null) compare their values. This pattern prevents crashes and correctly handles edge cases.

Algorithm for Safe Comparison:
1. Check: if a == null and b == null → return True
2. Check: if a == null or b == null → return False
3. Now safe to compare: if a.value == b.value → proceed, else return False

Why this order? If we compare values first and a is null, we get a null pointer error. If we check 'a == null or b == null' first, we might return False when both are null (which should be True). Hence the specific three-step check.

Time Complexity: O(1) for each comparison
Space Complexity: O(1)

Connection to L3: These comparison operations are used when navigating and examining tree nodes in L3.

Common Mistakes:
1. Comparing values before null checks → runtime error
2. Using wrong comparison operator (assignment = instead of equality ==)
3. Not handling the 'both null' case separately from 'one null' case
4. Forgetting that null == null is True

Real Code Pattern:
# Safe comparison pattern
def safe_compare(node1, node2):
    # Check both null first
    if node1 is None and node2 is None:
        return True
    # Check if one is null (but not both, since we already handled that)
    if node1 is None or node2 is None:
        return False
    # Now safe to compare values
    return node1.val == node2.val

# Individual operations:
is_both_null = node1 is None and node2 is None
is_one_null = (node1 is None) != (node2 is None)  # XOR
values_equal = node1.val == node2.val  # Only if both non-null

examples.trace:
Step 1: Check 'node1 is None and node2 is None' → True
Step 2: Return True immediately (don't check other conditions)

Step 1: Check both null → False (both are nodes)
Step 2: Check one null → False (neither is null)
Step 3: Compare values: 5 == 5 → True
Step 4: Return True

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Boolean Logic and Conditional Statements
ladders.desc: Understand AND/OR logic and if-else conditions for decision making.
ladders.explanation:
This foundational level covers the boolean logic and conditional statements that form the backbone of our comparison algorithm. At its core, tree comparison involves making decisions based on multiple conditions: we need to check if two nodes are both null, or if one is null, or if their values match, and combine these checks with AND/OR logic.

Intuition: Boolean logic allows us to express complex conditions simply. The tree comparison algorithm uses:
- AND (&&): All conditions must be True (e.g., left subtree matches AND right subtree matches)
- OR (||): At least one condition must be True (e.g., node1 is null OR node2 is null)
- NOT (!): Invert a condition
Conditional statements (if-elif-else) allow us to execute different code based on these conditions.

Key Concepts:
1. Boolean values: True and False
2. Comparison operators: == (equal), != (not equal)
3. Logical operators: AND, OR, NOT
4. Conditional execution: if, else, elif
5. Short-circuit evaluation: AND stops at first False, OR stops at first True

Basic Patterns:
1. Check multiple conditions: if condition1 and condition2:
2. Check alternative conditions: if condition1 or condition2:
3. Exclusive check: if condition1 != condition2 (XOR)
4. If-else chain: if... elif... else...

Time Complexity: O(1) for boolean operations
Space Complexity: O(1)

Connection to L4: The safe comparison in L4 uses these boolean operations to combine null checks and value comparisons.

Common Mistakes:
1. Using = (assignment) instead of == (comparison)
2. Confusing AND/OR precedence (AND usually evaluated before OR)
3. Not using parentheses to clarify complex expressions
4. Forgetting that 'if a and b:' requires both to be True

Real Code Pattern:
# Basic boolean operations
a = True
b = False

and_result = a and b  # False (both must be True)
or_result = a or b    # True (at least one True)
not_result = not a    # False (inverts True)

# Conditional statements
if a and b:
    print('Both true')
elif a or b:
    print('At least one true')
else:
    print('Both false')

# Comparison examples
x = 5
y = 10
is_equal = x == y  # False
not_equal = x != y  # True
greater = x > y  # False

# Combined conditions
if x > 0 and y > 0:  # Both must be positive
    print('Both positive')

if x == 5 or y == 5:  # At least one equals 5
    print('At least one is 5')

examples.trace:
Step 1: AND requires both operands to be True
Step 2: Left operand: True
Step 3: Right operand: False
Step 4: True AND False = False
Step 5: Result is False

Step 1: Evaluate first condition: 5 == 5 → True
Step 2: Evaluate second condition: 3 > 1 → True
Step 3: True AND True = True
Step 4: Result is True

approaches.name: Approach 2: Iterative Depth-First Search with Stack
approaches.summary: Use an explicit stack to simulate DFS, pushing pairs of nodes from both trees and comparing them iteratively. This avoids recursion overhead and is useful for deep trees to prevent stack overflow.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) in worst case (for skewed trees)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Iterative DFS Stack Solution for Tree Identity
ladders.desc: Complete implementation using explicit stack to compare two binary trees without recursion
ladders.explanation:
This solution implements the full problem of checking if two binary trees are identical using an iterative depth-first search approach with an explicit stack. The intuition is to simulate recursive DFS traversal without actual recursion by manually managing a stack data structure. This avoids potential stack overflow in deep trees while maintaining the same logical comparison flow as recursive DFS.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize an empty stack
2. Push the root node pair (node1, node2) onto the stack
3. While stack is not empty:
   a. Pop the top pair (n1, n2)
   b. If both are null: continue (both missing, so equal)
   c. If one is null and other isn't: return false (structural mismatch)
   d. If values differ: return false (value mismatch)
   e. Push right child pairs (n1.right, n2.right)
   f. Push left child pairs (n1.left, n2.left)
4. If loop completes without returning false, return true

Time complexity: O(n) where n is number of nodes in the smaller tree (we traverse all nodes once)
Space complexity: O(n) in worst case for skewed trees (stack holds all nodes)

Connection to level above: This is the complete solution that integrates all lower-level concepts into a working implementation.

Common mistakes:
1. Forgetting to check null cases before accessing node values
2. Pushing nodes in wrong order (should match DFS traversal order)
3. Not handling empty trees correctly
4. Using wrong comparison operators for node values

Real code pattern:
def are_identical_iterative(root1, root2):
    stack = [(root1, root2)]
    while stack:
        n1, n2 = stack.pop()
        if not n1 and not n2:
            continue
        if not n1 or not n2:
            return False
        if n1.val != n2.val:
            return False
        stack.append((n1.right, n2.right))
        stack.append((n1.left, n2.left))
    return True

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize stack with [(root1, root2)]
Step 2: Pop (1,1): values equal (1=1)
Step 3: Push (1.right,1.right) = (3,3)
Step 4: Push (1.left,1.left) = (2,2)
Step 5: Pop (2,2): values equal (2=2)
Step 6: Push (2.right,2.right) = (null,null)
Step 7: Push (2.left,2.left) = (null,null)
Step 8: Pop (null,null): both null, continue
Step 9: Pop (null,null): both null, continue
Step 10: Pop (3,3): values equal (3=3)
Step 11: Push (3.right,3.right) = (null,null)
Step 12: Push (3.left,3.left) = (null,null)
Step 13: Pop (null,null): both null, continue
Step 14: Pop (null,null): both null, continue
Step 15: Stack empty, return True

Step 1: Initialize stack with [(root1, root2)]
Step 2: Pop (1,1): values equal (1=1)
Step 3: Push (1.right,1.right) = (null,2)
Step 4: Push (1.left,1.left) = (2,null)
Step 5: Pop (2,null): one null, one not null → return False

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Stack-Based Node Pair Comparison Loop
ladders.desc: The main while loop that processes node pairs from stack and pushes children
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine represents the core iterative loop that drives the comparison process. The intuition is that we need to systematically visit every corresponding node pair in both trees in a depth-first order, comparing them as we go. By using a stack, we can manually control the traversal order while avoiding recursion.

Step-by-step algorithm for the loop:
1. While stack is not empty:
   a. Pop top pair (nodeA, nodeB)
   b. Handle the four possible null cases
   c. Compare node values if both non-null
   d. Push child pairs in reverse order (right then left) for DFS

Time complexity: O(n) for the loop itself
Space complexity: O(n) for stack storage

Connection to level above: This loop is the engine of the full solution - it contains all the comparison logic and traversal control.

Common mistakes:
1. Not using 'continue' when both nodes are null
2. Pushing children before checking if current nodes are valid
3. Wrong order of pushing children (should be right then left for pre-order DFS)
4. Not popping from stack correctly

Real code pattern:
while stack:
    n1, n2 = stack.pop()
    # Null handling
    if not n1 and not n2:
        continue
    if not n1 or not n2:
        return False
    # Value comparison
    if n1.val != n2.val:
        return False
    # Push children
    stack.append((n1.right, n2.right))
    stack.append((n1.left, n2.left))

examples.trace:
Step 1: stack = [(1,1)]
Step 2: pop (1,1)
Step 3: both not null, continue
Step 4: values equal (1=1)
Step 5: push (1.right,1.right)
Step 6: push (1.left,1.left)
Step 7: stack now has 2 pairs

Step 1: stack = [(null,5)]
Step 2: pop (null,5)
Step 3: one null, one not null
Step 4: return False immediately

ladders.title: Core Logic — Simultaneous Depth-First Traversal with Pair Management
ladders.desc: The technique of traversing two trees simultaneously using paired nodes
ladders.explanation:
This core logic involves the fundamental insight that to compare two trees for identity, we need to traverse them in sync - visiting corresponding nodes at exactly the same time. The intuition is that tree identity requires both structural and value equality, which can only be verified by comparing nodes that occupy the same position in both trees. By pairing nodes from both trees and processing these pairs, we ensure we're always comparing apples to apples.

Key insights:
1. We must compare nodes that are in the same position in their respective trees
2. The traversal order must be identical for both trees
3. We need to handle null nodes carefully as they represent missing structure
4. The comparison must check both existence (null vs non-null) and values

Algorithm concept:
- Treat (node_from_tree1, node_from_tree2) as a single unit
- Process pairs rather than individual nodes
- Maintain the invariant that we only compare nodes at corresponding positions

Time complexity: O(1) per pair comparison
Space complexity: O(1) per pair storage

Connection to level above: This pairing concept enables the stack-based approach - we push/pop pairs, not individual nodes.

Common mistakes:
1. Comparing nodes from different levels or positions
2. Not maintaining the pairing invariant throughout traversal
3. Treating null nodes as having values
4. Forgetting that structure matters as much as values

Real code pattern:
# The pair concept in action
pair = (tree1_node, tree2_node)
# Always process as a unit
if pair[0] and pair[1]:
    compare_values(pair[0].val, pair[1].val)
# Always push children as pairs
new_pair = (tree1_node.child, tree2_node.child)

examples.input: Tree1 node at position left-left, Tree2 node at same position
examples.trace:
Position: left child of left child
Tree1: follows left->left path to node A
Tree2: follows left->left path to node B
Pair: (A,B) - comparable because same position

examples.input: Tree1 has node at left-right, Tree2 has null at left-right
examples.trace:
Position: right child of left child
Tree1: follows left->right to node X
Tree2: follows left->right to null
Pair: (X,null) - mismatch because one exists, one doesn't

testCases.input: Tree1 doesn't have right, Tree2 does
Same values at root, different below

ladders.title: Building Block — Stack Operations for Tree Traversal
ladders.desc: Using stack data structure to implement depth-first tree traversal
ladders.explanation:
This building block focuses on how to use a stack (LIFO - Last In, First Out data structure) to traverse a tree in depth-first order without recursion. The intuition is that recursion implicitly uses the call stack, and we can explicitly simulate this with our own stack. For DFS, we want to go deep into the tree before exploring siblings, which matches the LIFO behavior of a stack.

Key concepts:
1. Stack follows LIFO: last item pushed is first popped
2. For pre-order DFS (root, left, right), we push right child before left child
3. Each stack element represents a node (or node pair) to process
4. The stack grows as we go deeper and shrinks as we backtrack

Algorithm for single tree DFS:
1. Push root onto stack
2. While stack not empty:
   a. Pop node
   b. Process node
   c. Push right child (if exists)
   d. Push left child (if exists)

Time complexity: O(n) to visit all nodes
Space complexity: O(h) where h is tree height

Connection to level above: This stack traversal technique enables the simultaneous traversal of two trees by pushing/poping pairs instead of single nodes.

Common mistakes:
1. Pushing children in wrong order (should be right then left for pre-order)
2. Forgetting to check if children exist before pushing
3. Not handling empty stack condition
4. Confusing stack (LIFO) with queue (FIFO)

Real code pattern:
stack = [root]
while stack:
    node = stack.pop()
    if node:
        process(node)
        # Push right first so left gets processed first (LIFO)
        if node.right:
            stack.append(node.right)
        if node.left:
            stack.append(node.left)

examples.trace:
Step 1: stack = [1]
Step 2: pop 1, process 1
Step 3: push 3 (right child)
Step 4: push 2 (left child)
Step 5: stack = [3,2]
Step 6: pop 2, process 2
Step 7: push 2's children (none)
Step 8: pop 3, process 3
Step 9: push 3's children (none)
Step 10: stack empty, done

Step 1: stack = [1]
Step 2: pop 1, process 1
Step 3: push 2 (right child)
Step 4: push null (left child) - will be ignored when popped
Step 5: stack = [2,null]
Step 6: pop null, ignore
Step 7: pop 2, process 2
Step 8: push 3 (right child of 2)
Step 9: push null (left child of 2)
Step 10: pop null, ignore
Step 11: pop 3, process 3
Step 12: stack empty, done

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Node Existence and Value Comparison
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations of checking if nodes exist and comparing their values
ladders.explanation:
This basic operation involves the fundamental checks needed when comparing two tree nodes: determining if nodes exist (null check) and if they do exist, comparing their values. The intuition is that tree identity has two components: structural identity (same shape) and value identity (same values at corresponding positions). We must check structure first because we cannot compare values of non-existent nodes.

Key operations:
1. Null check: Determine if a node reference points to an actual node or is null
2. Value access: Retrieve the value stored in a node (only if node exists)
3. Value comparison: Compare two values for equality

Step-by-step for comparing two nodes A and B:
1. Check if A is null AND B is null → both missing, so equal for structure
2. Check if A is null OR B is null → one exists, one doesn't, structural mismatch
3. Both exist: compare A.value == B.value

Time complexity: O(1) for each comparison
Space complexity: O(1)

Connection to level above: These basic comparisons are what happens inside the stack processing loop for each node pair.

Common mistakes:
1. Comparing values before checking for null (causes null pointer error)
2. Using wrong comparison operator (== for equality, not =)
3. Not handling all four null cases (null,null), (null,node), (node,null), (node,node)
4. Forgetting that two nulls are considered equal for tree structure

Real code pattern:
def compare_nodes(node1, node2):
    # Case 1: Both null
    if node1 is None and node2 is None:
        return True  # Structurally equal
    # Case 2: One null, one not
    if node1 is None or node2 is None:
        return False  # Structurally different
    # Case 3: Both exist, compare values
    return node1.val == node2.val

examples.input: Node A: null, Node B: null
examples.trace:
Step 1: Check if A is null → yes
Step 2: Check if B is null → yes
Step 3: Both null → return True
No value comparison needed

examples.input: Node A: Node(5), Node B: Node(5)
examples.trace:
Step 1: Check if A is null → no
Step 2: Check if B is null → no
Step 3: Both not null, proceed to value comparison
Step 4: A.val = 5, B.val = 5
Step 5: 5 == 5 → return True

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure and References
ladders.desc: Understanding how binary tree nodes are structured and referenced
ladders.explanation:
This foundation concept covers the basic building block of binary trees: the node. A binary tree node is a simple data structure that contains a value and references (pointers) to at most two child nodes. The intuition is that trees are recursive structures where each node can be seen as the root of its own subtree. Understanding nodes is fundamental because all tree operations ultimately work with individual nodes.

Node structure typically includes:
1. Value/data: The information stored at this node (can be any data type)
2. Left child reference: Pointer to left child node (or null if no left child)
3. Right child reference: Pointer to right child node (or null if no right child)

Key concepts:
1. Node reference: A variable that points to (holds the memory address of) a node
2. Null reference: A special value indicating no node (often called None, null, or nil)
3. Child nodes: Nodes referenced by this node's left/right pointers
4. Leaf node: A node with both children null

Basic operations:
1. Creating a node: node = Node(value)
2. Setting left child: node.left = other_node
3. Setting right child: node.right = other_node
4. Checking if node exists: node is not None
5. Accessing value: node.val

Time complexity: O(1) for all basic node operations
Space complexity: O(1) per node

Connection to level above: All tree comparisons work by examining and comparing individual nodes, so understanding nodes is prerequisite.

Common beginner mistakes:
1. Confusing the node itself with its value
2. Not understanding that child references can be null
3. Trying to access properties of null references
4. Not realizing trees are built from linked nodes, not arrays

Real code pattern:
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0):
        self.val = val
        self.left = None
        self.right = None

# Creating a simple tree
root = TreeNode(1)
root.left = TreeNode(2)
root.right = TreeNode(3)

examples.output: Node object with val=5, left=None, right=None
examples.trace:
Step 1: Allocate memory for new node
Step 2: Set node.val = 5
Step 3: Set node.left = None (default)
Step 4: Set node.right = None (default)
Step 5: Result is an isolated node with no children

Step 1: Create root node: root = TreeNode(1)
Step 2: Create left child: left_child = TreeNode(2)
Step 3: Connect them: root.left = left_child
Step 4: root.right remains None
Step 5: Structure: root(1) has left→(2), right→null

testCases.expected: Node with both children not null

approaches.name: Approach 3: Iterative Breadth-First Search with Queue
approaches.summary: Use a queue to perform level-order traversal, comparing nodes level by level from both trees. This approach processes nodes in a breadth-first manner, which can be intuitive for visualizing tree structure.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) in worst case (for full trees)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Iterative BFS Tree Identity Check
ladders.desc: Complete solution using iterative BFS with queue to check if two binary trees are identical.
ladders.explanation: This solution implements a breadth-first search using a queue to compare two binary trees for structural and value identity. The intuition is that trees are identical if they have the same structure and node values when traversed level by level. We use a queue to process nodes in pairs (one from each tree) in the order they appear at each level. Starting with the root nodes, we enqueue child nodes for comparison in subsequent iterations. If at any point we encounter mismatched values (one node is null while the other isn't, or values differ), we return false. The algorithm terminates when the queue is empty, indicating all nodes have been processed and matched, returning true. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize a queue and enqueue the root nodes of both trees as a pair. 2. While the queue is not empty: a. Dequeue a pair of nodes. b. If both nodes are null, continue to next iteration (both trees have missing nodes at this position). c. If one node is null and the other isn't, return false (structural mismatch). d. If node values differ, return false (value mismatch). e. Enqueue the left children as a pair and right children as a pair. 3. If the loop completes without returning false, return true. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the number of nodes in the smaller tree (we traverse each node once). Space complexity is O(n) in worst case for a full tree where the queue holds all nodes at the widest level. This connects to the parent problem by providing a complete, efficient solution that handles all edge cases. Common mistakes include not handling null nodes properly (e.g., forgetting to check for one null while the other isn't), or enqueuing children without pairing them correctly. Real code pattern: def isSameTree(p, q): queue = [(p, q)] while queue: node1, node2 = queue.pop(0) if not node1 and not node2: continue if not node1 or not node2: return False if node1.val != node2.val: return False queue.append((node1.left, node2.left)) queue.append((node1.right, node2.right)) return True

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize queue with (1,1). Queue: [(1,1)]. Step 2: Dequeue (1,1). Both nodes exist, values match (1==1). Enqueue left children (2,2) and right children (3,3). Queue: [(2,2),(3,3)]. Step 3: Dequeue (2,2). Values match (2==2). Enqueue left children (null,null) and right children (null,null). Queue: [(3,3),(null,null),(null,null)]. Step 4: Dequeue (3,3). Values match (3==3). Enqueue left and right children as (null,null) pairs. Queue: [(null,null),(null,null),(null,null),(null,null)]. Step 5: Dequeue (null,null). Both null, continue. Repeat for all remaining null pairs. Step 6: Queue empty, return true.

Step 1: Initialize queue with (1,1). Queue: [(1,1)]. Step 2: Dequeue (1,1). Values match (1==1). Enqueue left children (2,null) and right children (null,2). Queue: [(2,null),(null,2)]. Step 3: Dequeue (2,null). One node is 2, other is null → structural mismatch. Return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BFS Node Pair Comparison Loop
ladders.desc: The core loop that dequeues node pairs and compares them, enqueuing children for further processing.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the BFS approach, handling the iterative comparison of nodes from two trees. The intuition is that by processing nodes in pairs from a queue, we can simulate simultaneous level-order traversal of both trees. At each step, we check if the current pair of nodes matches in structure and value, and if so, we schedule their children for future comparison. This ensures we compare trees level by level, left to right. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. While the queue is not empty: a. Dequeue a pair (node1, node2). b. If both nodes are null, skip to next iteration (compatible missing nodes). c. If exactly one node is null, return false (structural difference). d. If values differ, return false. e. Enqueue (node1.left, node2.left) and (node1.right, node2.right). 2. If loop completes, all nodes matched. Time complexity per iteration is O(1) for dequeuing and comparisons, but overall O(n) for n nodes. Space complexity is O(n) for the queue. This feeds into L0 by providing the main processing logic; L0 adds initialization and return handling. Common mistakes include not using a proper queue (e.g., using a stack leads to DFS), or comparing values before checking null conditions. Real code pattern: while queue: n1, n2 = queue.pop(0) if not n1 and not n2: continue if not n1 or not n2: return False if n1.val != n2.val: return False queue.append((n1.left, n2.left)) queue.append((n1.right, n2.right))

examples.input: Queue initially: [(A,B)] where A.val=1, B.val=1, A.left=2, B.left=2, A.right=3, B.right=3
examples.output: Queue after processing: [(2,2),(3,3)] and no false returned
examples.trace: Step 1: Dequeue (A,B). Both not null, values match (1==1). Enqueue left children (2,2) and right children (3,3). New queue: [(2,2),(3,3)]. Step 2: Dequeue (2,2). Values match (2==2). Enqueue their children (null,null) pairs. Queue: [(3,3),(null,null),(null,null)]. Continue similarly.

examples.input: Queue initially: [(X,Y)] where X.val=1, Y.val=1, X.left=2, Y.left=null
examples.trace: Step 1: Dequeue (X,Y). Both not null, values match (1==1). Enqueue left children (2,null) and right children (null,null). Queue: [(2,null),(null,null)]. Step 2: Dequeue (2,null). One node is 2 (not null), other is null → return false.

testCases.input: Queue: [(1,1)] where both nodes have value 1
Queue: [(A,B)] where A.val=3, B.val=3, children all null
Queue: [(A,B)] where A.val=4, B.val=4, A.left=5, B.left=5
Queue: [(A,B)] where A.val=6, B.val=6, A.right=7, B.right=8
testCases.expected: Enqueue children but will fail later when comparing 7 and 8

testCases.input: Queue: [(A,B)] where A.val=9, B.val=9, A.left=10, B.left=null
Queue: [(A,B)] where both null after several iterations

ladders.title: Core Logic — Simultaneous Level-order Traversal with Pairing
ladders.desc: The technique of traversing two trees level by level while keeping nodes in sync using paired queue entries.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves the insight that to compare two trees for identity, we can traverse them simultaneously in level-order (breadth-first) while maintaining correspondence between nodes. By pairing nodes from each tree in the queue, we ensure that when we dequeue, we compare nodes that occupy the same position in their respective trees. This mirrors how we visually compare trees: check roots, then left children, then right children, moving level by level. The intuition is that if trees are identical, every pair of nodes dequeued will match in null status and value, and their children will be enqueued in the same order. The step-by-step process: 1. Always enqueue and dequeue pairs (node_from_tree1, node_from_tree2). 2. Process pairs in FIFO order to maintain level-order. 3. When enqueuing children, pair left children together and right children together. This ensures structural alignment. Time and space are O(n) as we may process all nodes. This connects to L1 by providing the traversal strategy; L1 implements the detailed comparisons. Common mistakes include enqueuing nodes individually rather than in pairs, which loses synchronization. Real code pattern: Use a queue of tuples, e.g., queue = [(p, q)] initially, then always append pairs like (node1.left, node2.left).

examples.input: Trees with roots A and B, both value 1, left children value 2, right children value 3
examples.trace: Start: Queue has pair (A,B). After processing: Dequeue (A,B), since they match, enqueue their left children as a pair (2,2) and right children as (3,3). Now queue has (2,2) and (3,3). Next, dequeue (2,2), enqueue their children (null pairs), etc. This keeps nodes paired by position.

examples.input: Tree1: root 1 with left child 2, Tree2: root 1 with right child 2
examples.output: Mismatch detected when comparing children pairs
examples.trace: Start: Queue (1,1). Dequeue: values match. Enqueue left children: (2,null) for Tree1's left and Tree2's left (null). Enqueue right children: (null,2). Queue now: [(2,null),(null,2)]. Next, dequeue (2,null): one null, one not → mismatch. The pairing reveals structural difference.

testCases.input: Pair (root1, root2) where both roots are null
testCases.expected: Enqueue no children, queue becomes empty

testCases.input: Pair (node1, node2) where node1 has left child, node2 has left child
Pair (node1, node2) where node1 has left child, node2 has no left child
Pair (node1, node2) where both have no children
Pair where nodes are from different levels implicitly
testCases.expected: Will cause mismatch if not aligned

testCases.input: Enqueuing children in order left then right
Pair with one node having two children, other having one
testCases.expected: Enqueue pairs that will reveal mismatch

ladders.title: Building Block — Queue Operations for Tree Traversal
ladders.desc: Using a queue to manage nodes in FIFO order for level-order (BFS) tree traversal.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on how to use a queue data structure to traverse a tree in breadth-first order. The intuition is that a queue (First-In-First-Out) allows us to process nodes in the order they are discovered, which naturally corresponds to level-order: we start with the root, then its children, then grandchildren, etc. By enqueuing children when we visit a node, and dequeuing the next node to visit, we ensure all nodes at a given level are processed before moving to the next level. This is fundamental to BFS. The step-by-step algorithm for traversing a single tree: 1. Initialize queue with root node. 2. While queue not empty: a. Dequeue a node. b. Process it (e.g., print value). c. Enqueue its left child (if not null). d. Enqueue its right child (if not null). Time complexity is O(n) as each node is enqueued and dequeued once. Space complexity is O(n) for the queue in worst case. This connects to L2 by providing the traversal mechanism; L2 extends it to handle two trees simultaneously. Common mistakes include using a stack (LIFO) instead of queue, which results in DFS, or not handling null children properly. Real code pattern: queue = [root] while queue: node = queue.pop(0) if node.left: queue.append(node.left) if node.right: queue.append(node.right)

examples.input: Tree: [1,2,3] where 1 is root, 2 left child, 3 right child
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue: [1]. Step 2: Dequeue 1, process 1. Enqueue left child 2 and right child 3. Queue: [2,3]. Step 3: Dequeue 2, process 2. Enqueue its children (null, so skip). Queue: [3]. Step 4: Dequeue 3, process 3. Enqueue its children (skip). Queue empty, done.

examples.input: Tree: [1,2,null,3] where root 1, left child 2, left-left child 3
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue: [1]. Step 2: Dequeue 1, process 1. Enqueue left 2, right null (skip). Queue: [2]. Step 3: Dequeue 2, process 2. Enqueue left 3, right null. Queue: [3]. Step 4: Dequeue 3, process 3. Enqueue children (skip). Queue empty.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Node Comparison and Null Checking
ladders.desc: Comparing two tree nodes for value equality while handling null cases.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves checking if two tree nodes are identical, which requires comparing their values and handling cases where one or both nodes might be null. The intuition is that for trees to be identical, corresponding nodes must either both be null (missing in both trees) or both be non-null with the same value. If one is null and the other isn't, that indicates a structural difference. This is a fundamental step in tree algorithms. The step-by-step process: 1. Check if both nodes are null: if yes, they match (return true or continue). 2. Check if exactly one is null: if yes, they don't match (return false). 3. Compare the values: if equal, they match; otherwise, don't match. Time complexity is O(1) per comparison. Space is O(1). This connects to L3 by being a key operation during traversal; L3 uses it when processing dequeued nodes. Common mistakes include comparing values before checking nulls (leading to null pointer errors), or not considering the case where both are null as a match. Real code pattern: if not node1 and not node2: return True if not node1 or not node2: return False return node1.val == node2.val

examples.trace: Step 1: Both null → match. No value comparison needed.
Step 1: Both not null. Step 2: Neither null. Step 3: Values equal (5==5) → match.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure
ladders.desc: Understanding the basic building block of a binary tree: nodes with value, left child, and right child.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces a binary tree node, which is the fundamental unit of a binary tree. Each node contains a value (e.g., an integer) and references to its left and right children, which can be other nodes or null. The intuition is that a binary tree is a hierarchical structure where each node has at most two children, forming a rooted tree. This is essential for representing hierarchical data like expressions, directories, or any parent-child relationships. The node structure typically includes: value (data stored), left (pointer to left child), right (pointer to right child). In code, this is often a class or struct. For example, in Python: class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None): self.val = val; self.left = left; self.right = right. Time and space are O(1) per node. This connects to L4 by providing the objects being compared; L4 operates on these nodes. Common mistakes include not initializing children as null by default, or confusing the order of children. Real code pattern: Define a simple class with val, left, and right attributes.

examples.input: Create a node with value 1 and no children
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize node object. Step 2: Set val to 1. Step 3: Set left to None (null). Step 4: Set right to None. Result is a leaf node.

examples.input: Create a node with value 2, left child value 3, right child null
examples.trace: Step 1: Create node for value 2. Step 2: Create another node for value 3. Step 3: Set left of node 2 to point to node 3. Step 4: Set right of node 2 to null. Now node 2 has a left child.

testCases.input: Node with value 1 and left child value 2
Node with value 3 and right child value 4
Node with value 5, left child 6, right child 7
Node with value 8 and no children (leaf)
Node with value 9, left child null, right child null
Node with value 10, left child Node(11), right child null
Node with value 12, left child null, right child Node(13)
Node with value 14, left child Node(15), right child Node(16)
Node with value 17 and children that are themselves nodes with values
